label: Narrow-shell 2BR / 1 bath, FRONT-BACK split, no courtyard, no hall — great_room + kitchen occupy the front, master + standard + common bath sit side by side across the rear, each dooring directly across the boundary wall. Revised from an earlier courtyard version at the user's request: the open-air light well is removed and the boundary simply closes up.

adjacencies.note: master and standard sit side by side across the rear band — shared wall only, no door.
common bath sits at the far end of the rear band, next to standard — shared wall only, no door.
master's front wall = great's rear wall at the boundary — direct door, no hall.
standard's front wall overlaps great's rear wall — direct door. 1.2 m floor gives 0.8 m clear door + frame each side.

common bath's door opens into great — DEFAULT host of the 'common_access' door-host group. Both bedrooms reach it by stepping out into the public zone and back in, same idiom as squarish's side_split_bath_gr, rotated 90 degrees for the narrow shell.
shared plumbing wall — common bath sits behind kitchen's edge. ALTERNATE host of 'common_access': door placed here instead of great when this wall >= 1.5 m.
great_room + kitchen form one open public band at the front — no separate dining room in this variant.

soft_proximities.note: pull common toward standard's wall when there's slack.

lot_adjustment_profiles.name: compact_nw_front_back_split_bath
lot_adjustment_profiles.rationale: This layout needs master + standard + common bath to fit SIDE BY SIDE across the rear band, which is the one thing a narrow lot has the least of (width). Both bedrooms are capped on least-dim so the solver doesn't try to widen them at the expense of feasibility; extra buildable width should go to depth/area instead where possible.

setback_elements.note: kitchen is in the front band here (near the street), same consequence as 1s_2br_sq_front_back_split_baths_ds_lk — no rear wall behind kitchen to extend a service tail from, so dirty kitchen moves to the side setback on kitchen's side instead.

notes: Narrow-shell catalog topology — front-back split, revised at the user's request to remove the open-air courtyard from the original concept. Without the courtyard as a buffer, the public/private boundary simply closes into a single wall, and every private room doors directly across it into great_room — same circulation idiom as 1s_2br_sq_front_back_split_baths_ds_lk, minus the ensuite (single common bath here, matching the simpler 1-bath program used across all three narrow options).
Layout intent (canonical form, front/street at the bottom):

IMPORTANT FEASIBILITY CAVEAT: this is the least natural fit of the three narrow options. Options 1 and 2 both stack rooms front-to-rear, which leans into a narrow lot's real advantage — depth is abundant, width is scarce. This option does the opposite: it needs master, standard, AND common bath to fit side by side across ONE row of width. On a true 6 m-frontage lot that's very tight (three rooms in maybe 4.5-5 m of buildable width). Recommend treating this as the option that wants the WIDER end of 'narrow' (8 m+ frontage) rather than the narrowest lots — or treat it as a candidate for a future 2-story version, where the rear band's footprint only has to hold one bedroom per floor instead of two side by side.

Removing the courtyard also removes its light/ventilation benefit for the middle of the house — worth keeping in mind if the eventual buildable envelope puts common bath or an interior wall far from both the front and rear exterior walls.
No carport declared — side setbacks on a narrow lot rarely have room for one; see 1s_2br_nw_front_carport_spine_bath.json for the front-loaded carport pattern, which could be adapted here too.
No fallback declared yet — first draft.
